verbGlossary.OPERATES: {source} resource controls and runs {target} to perform its intended construction function, requiring specific training or certification.
verbGlossary.STAGES: {source} resource assembles, positions, and secures {target} at the work location so other trades can use it safely.
verbGlossary.MONITORS: {source} or quality professional observes {target}'s work activities to verify compliance with safety plans, OSHA regulations, and site-specific requirements.
verbGlossary.INSPECTS: {source} professional examines and verifies {target}'s completed work against specifications, approved submittals, and code requirements before it is covered or accepted.
verbGlossary.COMMISSIONS: {source} agent verifies {target}'s installed systems perform to design intent through functional testing, test-and-balance, and integrated system checks.
verbGlossary.LAYS_OUT_FOR: {source} establishes control points, reference lines, and elevation marks that the receiving {target} uses to position their work accurately.
verbGlossary.ASSISTS: General {target} provides manual support to the skilled trade — moving materials, holding components, cleaning work areas, and performing preparatory tasks.
verbGlossary.SUPPLEMENTS: {source} serves as a secondary or backup resource that extends the capability or capacity of the primary {target} on site.
verbGlossary.LOADS: Earthmoving {target} fills hauling equipment with excavated material, spoil, or aggregate at the dig face or stockpile.
verbGlossary.PRECEDES: {source} completes its operation before the next resource can begin — establishing a sequential dependency in {target}ion chain.
verbGlossary.POWERS: Temporary utilities supply electrical power, compressed air, or fuel to the dependent {target} so it can operate before permanent building services are available.
verbGlossary.EMBEDDED_IN: Reinforcing {target} is placed inside the host material before it cures or sets, becoming permanently bonded to provide tensile or structural capacity.
verbGlossary.FORMS_FOR: {source} is assembled as temporary formwork that shapes and contains {target} during placement and curing, then removed.
verbGlossary.COMPOSITES_WITH: Two {target}s are combined into a composite structural system where each contributes complementary properties — typically steel for tension and concrete for compression.
verbGlossary.REINFORCED_BY: {source} receives reinforcing elements placed in its cores, joints, or cavities to increase structural capacity beyond the base {target} alone.
verbGlossary.LAYERED_WITH: {target}s are installed in direct contact as a multi-layer assembly where each layer provides a distinct performance function — typically waterproofing, insulation, and membrane.
verbGlossary.APPLIED_TO: {source} is adhered, sprayed, or sheet-applied to the surface of the host {target} to provide moisture protection or surface treatment.
verbGlossary.FILLED_WITH: Cavities or stud bays in the framing {target} are packed with insulation or fill material to achieve thermal, acoustic, or fire-resistance performance.
verbGlossary.COORDINATED_WITH: Two {target}s or material systems must coordinate their rough-in locations, routing, and installation sequence to avoid conflicts and share limited ceiling or wall cavity space.
verbGlossary.INSULATED_BY: Distribution {target} is wrapped or enclosed by thermal insulation to prevent condensation, heat loss, or heat gain in the conveyed medium.
verbGlossary.FEEDS: Electrical con{target}or material supplies power or signal current to the connected system, making it operational.
verbGlossary.CONNECTED_BY: {source} is joined to its distribution network through {target}work, piping, or conduit that delivers or returns the working medium.
verbGlossary.POWERED_BY: {source} or system requires electrical {target} from the conductor material for motor drive, controls, and operational functions.
verbGlossary.COMBINED_WITH: {target}s are mixed, blended, or placed together to form a unified site-work or structural element such as aggregate base, fill, or paving.
verbGlossary.COORDINATES_WITH: Two {target}s share overlapping work zones, require sequential access, or must align their installations to avoid spatial conflicts — typically during MEP rough-in.
